text: The surgeon begins an ambulatory laparoscopic myomectomy for a nine-centimeter myoma by controlling both uterine arteries at their origin. The peritoneum is opened between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, and the retroperitoneal space is dissected to identify classical landmarks, including the clearly visible ureter medially.

The surgeon develops the space lateral to the ureter, revealing the internal iliac artery. The pararectal fossa is opened between the ureter and the internal iliac artery, with the surgeon following the internal iliac artery anteriorly.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated uterine artery along the utero-umbilical tract and places a clip on the first medial branch, the uterine artery, after medializing the left ureter. The same procedure is performed on the right side with medial traction applied to the IP ligament.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing the ureteral rotation. The peritoneum is tracked to expose the avascular retroperitoneal space, allowing visualization of the right ureter.

The surgeon follows the ureter tail to reach the internal iliac artery, developing the lateral pararectal fossa. The uterine artery is visible medially, and the surgeon tracks it to develop the space above and below for safe clip placement.
The surgeon places a clip on the uterine artery at its origin, achieving vascular control of the uterus.

The surgeon performs a longitudinal incision above the relief of the posterior myoma, which is a type II-V myoma measuring nine centimeters. Using the harmonic ACE device, the uterine serosa is opened until the myometrial capsule is reached.
The surgical field appears bloodless due to effective vascular control, as the surgeon proceeds to cut tissue using a surgical instrument.

The surgeon uses a ten millimeter forceps placed in the left iliac trocar to grasp and retract the myoma from its bed. The surrounding myometrial tissue is carefully cut, allowing for the progressive extraction of the large myoma.

The surgeon applies ultrasonic energy directly on the myoma, avoiding the healthy myometrium to minimize the risk of post-operative complications. The correct dissecting plane is identified, facilitating a straightforward myomectomy.
The surgeon uses a 14mm Vigrin One stitch to close the myoma bed, placing stitches three centimeters outside the uterine scar to ensure quick and safe closure with excellent hemostasis.

The surgeon tracks the stitch and invaginates the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, using an intra-corporeal knotting technique to close the defect. The first knot effectively controls over eighty percent of the hemostasis, with the closure extending three centimeters outside the uterine scar.

The surgeon takes a large bite of myometrial tissue, ensuring the proximal part of the fallopian tube is not included in the stitch. The intracorporeal knotting technique is used to secure the tissue, repeating the same procedure as previously described.

The surgeon applies traction on both stitches, visibly invaginating the healthy myometrium completely inside the myoma bed, which aids in reducing the risk of post-operative hematoma and fever, and promotes strong cicatrization.
The surgeon continues closing the myoma bed using stitches, with four or five stitches being sufficient for a nine-centimeter myoma.
The surgeon continues using the intracorporeal knotting technique to close the myoma bed, ensuring effective hemostasis and secure closure of the myometrial defect.

The surgeon completes the final intracorporeal knot, achieving a perfect anatomical reconstruction of the uterus. The stitches result in blanching near the uterine scar, indicating successful hemostasis. Care is taken to avoid damaging the nearby fallopian tube during this maneuver.

The uterine reconstruction is complete, and the surgeon prepares for morcellation using a 15mm morcellator positioned in the left iliac fossa, set at 700 RPM. The operative trocar is closed to reduce the risk of incisional hernia.
The final aspect of the surgery is shown, with the pelvis being cleaned thoroughly to ensure a clear operative field.
